profile.name: Thảo Nguyên Trần
profile.title: Data Analyst · Risk Management · Quantitative Research
profile.tagline: Mathematical Economics graduate bridging quantitative analysis and high-stakes financial decision-making. Specialized in credit risk modeling, fraud detection, and statistical early-warning systems. Turning complex data into defensible risk judgments for banking and financial institutions.
profile.bio: Trained in Mathematical Economics with hands-on research across credit risk, banking performance, and market risk modeling. I use statistical and machine learning methods including panel data regression, Bayesian inference, and classification models to quantify uncertainty and support high-stakes decisions in finance, risk management, and economic policy. My work spans published research, regulatory-aligned frameworks (Basel II/III, ISO 31000), and production-ready risk pipelines.

about.professional_focus: Risk Analysis, Data Analytics, and Business Intelligence — with emphasis on statistical modeling, machine learning, and quantitative research.

projects.title: Credit Risk Analysis at Agribank: Classification Techniques for Fraud Detection
projects.type: Graduation Internship Report · Agribank Saigon Branch · 2025
projects.context: Vietnam's rapid credit expansion has intensified fraud risk in personal credit portfolios. This study addresses a critical gap: Agribank Saigon Branch lacked an automated, data-driven early-warning system for credit card fraud detection, relying instead on manual review processes vulnerable to human error.
projects.researchQuestion: Which classification technique — Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, or Random Forest — best detects credit card fraud in a highly imbalanced banking dataset, and how can class imbalance be mitigated without sacrificing recall on the minority (fraud) class?

dataset.source: Agribank Saigon Branch internal transaction system

methodology: Exploratory Data Analysis: outlier detection via scatter plots, transaction time distribution, class imbalance visualization
Data Preprocessing: IQR-based outlier handling, Robust Scaler normalization (median-centered, IQR-scaled) to resist outlier influence
Correlation analysis: heatmap of 30 features to identify multicollinearity and key fraud predictors
Train/Test Split: 80/20 stratified split — 227,845 training / 56,962 testing transactions
Class Imbalance Handling: SMOTE applied to training set only — balanced to 227,454 samples per class
Models trained: Logistic Regression (baseline), Decision Tree (max depth = 16), Random Forest (100 trees, majority voting)
Evaluation metrics: Accuracy, Precision, Recall (primary), F1-Score, Confusion Matrix

models.name: Logistic Regression (baseline, no SMOTE)
models.note: High accuracy but 37% of fraudulent transactions missed — unacceptable for fraud detection

models.name: Logistic Regression (with SMOTE)
models.note: High recall but 1,393 false positives — operationally costly

models.name: Decision Tree (with SMOTE)
models.note: Improved balance — fewer false positives than LR with SMOTE

models.name: Random Forest (with SMOTE) — Best Model
models.note: Best overall — only 10 false positives, highest precision-recall balance

results: Random Forest achieved 99.95% accuracy with 84.16% recall and 89.47% precision on fraud class — best balance across all models
SMOTE oversampling raised fraud recall from 63% (baseline) to 94% in Logistic Regression, but at the cost of 1,393 false positives
Random Forest produced only 10 false positives vs 1,393 for Logistic Regression — critical for operational efficiency
Payment history and collateral assets identified as the top 2 predictors of credit risk via feature correlation analysis
PCA dimension reduction (15 to 8 features) retained 95% of original information while improving model efficiency
keyFindings: Class imbalance (fraud = 0.172%) is the central challenge — accuracy alone is a misleading metric in fraud detection
SMOTE effectively addresses recall at the cost of precision; Random Forest naturally handles imbalance better than linear models
Robust Scaler outperforms Standard Scaler for financial transaction data due to outlier resistance
Random Forest ensemble structure (100 trees, bootstrap sampling, Gini impurity splitting) provides superior generalization
Proposed 3-tier alert system for Agribank: auto-approve (high confidence legitimate), flag for review (borderline), auto-block (high confidence fraud)
figures: Fig 4.1: Scatter plot — Amount vs Time showing outlier transactions above $10,000
Fig 4.2: Pie chart — 99.83% legitimate vs 0.17% fraudulent transactions
Fig 4.3: Heatmap — full 31x31 feature correlation matrix
Fig 4.4: Density plot — transaction time distribution showing daytime peak activity
Fig 4.5: Heatmap — post-scaling correlation matrix with scaled_amount and scaled_time
Fig 4.6: Confusion matrix — baseline Logistic Regression (no SMOTE)
Fig 4.7: Diagram — undersampling vs oversampling illustration
Fig 4.8/4.9: Bar charts — class distribution before and after SMOTE
Fig 4.11: Confusion matrix — Logistic Regression with SMOTE
Fig 4.12: Confusion matrix — Decision Tree with SMOTE
Fig 4.13: Confusion matrix — Random Forest with SMOTE (best result)
projects.supervisor: Assoc. Prof. Pham Hoang Uyen, University of Economics and Law VNU-HCM

projects.title: Walmart India E-commerce: Data Visualization & Storytelling
projects.type: Team Project (7 members) · UEL · Data Visualization Course · 2024
projects.context: E-commerce platforms generate massive behavioral data that remains underutilized without effective visualization. This project analyzed Walmart India customer data to extract actionable business insights — translating raw transaction records into strategic recommendations across demographics, products, payments, and geography.
projects.researchQuestion: What does Walmart India's customer data reveal about purchasing behavior across demographics, product categories, payment methods, and geography — and how can these insights drive strategic business decisions?

dataset.source: Kaggle open dataset — Walmart India customer transactions
dataset.target: Customer purchasing behavior and revenue patterns
dataset.features: CID, TID, Gender, Age Group, Purchase Date, Product Category, Discount, Payment Method, Location, Net Amount

methodology: Data Collection: Kaggle dataset — 14 fields, ~21,000 transactions across India
Data Preprocessing: Pandas for cleaning, type conversion, null handling, date parsing
Demographic Analysis: Gender distribution, age group segmentation, revenue by cohort
Product Analysis: Category revenue ranking, seasonal trends, discount utilization by category
Payment Analysis: Transaction method distribution, UPI vs card vs COD breakdown
Geographic Analysis: City-level revenue mapping, top vs emerging markets identification
Storytelling: 3 buyer personas (Khari/Nayar/Sam) woven through data narrative

keyInsights.finding: Nearly equal split: Female 33.6%, Male 32.9%, Other 33.5%
keyInsights.implication: Platform has broad inclusive appeal — gender-neutral marketing opportunity

keyInsights.finding: 25-45 age group: 60M+ INR revenue — 3x the 45-60 group
keyInsights.implication: Core segment; targeted loyalty programs would yield highest ROI
Stock and promotion priority: Electronics first, then Clothing
Invest in UPI incentives — fastest-growing segment

keyInsights.finding: Mumbai 32M INR, Delhi 31M INR. Dehradun, Srinagar under 2M
keyInsights.implication: Tier-2 cities: untapped growth markets for localized campaigns

keyInsights.finding: December peak: 18M INR. February lowest: 10M INR
keyInsights.implication: Front-load inventory in Q4; use Feb for clearance promotions

results: Electronics dominates with 441K INR net sales — 30% ahead of Clothing (339K INR)
Age 25-45 generates 60M+ INR revenue — 3x the next cohort (45-60 at 20M INR)
December peak at 18M INR vs February low at 10M — 80% seasonal swing
Credit Card leads at 40%+ of transactions; UPI wallets collectively at 14.6% and growing
Mumbai (32M) and Delhi (31M) account for 40%+ of total platform revenue
SEASONALOFFER21 drove 15,000 purchases in Electronics alone — highest discount engagement
keyFindings: Gender parity (33/33/33) is rare in e-commerce — signals genuinely inclusive platform design
25-45 cohort drives 60% of revenue despite being 33% of users — classic Pareto pattern
December 80% above February baseline — seasonal strategy is the highest-leverage lever
UPI growing at 14.6% — digital wallet adoption will overtake debit cards within 2 years
Tier-2 cities (Dehradun, Srinagar) under 2M INR — untapped with high growth ceiling
projects.supervisor: PhD. Nguyen Phuc Son, University of Economics and Law VNU-HCM

projects.title: Internal Banking IT Applications & Credit Risk: Ridge Regression Analysis
projects.context: Investigated how Core Banking, basic IT applications, and e-payment systems affect credit risk in Vietnamese commercial banks.
methodology: 121 observations from Vietnam ICT Index (2017–2022)
3 independent variables: Core Banking, Basic IT, E-Payment Index
Ridge Regression (lambda = 0.156)
Feature Importance via Mean Dropout Loss
results: Validation MSE = 0.423, Test MSE = 0.363, MAE = 0.457
Core Banking & IT applications reduced credit risk (negative coefficients)
E-payment increased credit risk (beta = +0.206) — critical policy finding
Basic IT Application had highest feature importance (0.837)

projects.title: AI Impact on the Sharing Economy: GSCA Structural Analysis
projects.context: Investigated how AI transforms sharing economy models (transportation, accommodation, services) and which sectors benefit most.
methodology: 12 AI application variables x 3 benefit variables
300 students surveyed, 250 valid responses
McDonald's Omega reliability test
EFA with ULS & Promax rotation + GSCA modeling
results: Model fit: GFI = 0.973 (above 0.93), SRMR = 0.046 (below 0.08)
Shared transportation AI: beta = 0.385 (strongest impact)
All paths statistically significant at 95% confidence
Schedule optimization (0.727) & device sharing (0.748) showed highest AI value

projects.title: Credit Risk in Vietnam's Commercial Banks: Panel Data Regression
projects.context: Analyzed factors influencing credit risk across 27 Vietnamese commercial banks. Credit risk represents ~54% of total banking system risk.
methodology: 272 observations from 27 banks (2012–2022)
11 variables: past credit risk, bank size, capital, GDP, inflation, unemployment
OLS, FEM, REM comparison + GLS correction
Hausman Test, VIF checks
results: Lagged credit risk beta = 0.496 (strongest positive predictor)
Inflation beta = 0.0016, Unemployment beta = 0.0025 (both positive)
Bank size beta = -0.0014 (larger banks lower risk)
Policy solutions proposed based on Basel II & III frameworks

projects.title: Stock Market Bubble Detection: Multifractal Analysis + Behavioral Signals
projects.context: VN-Index crashed 35% in 6 months in 2018 and 2022. Can we detect bubbles earlier by combining market complexity with behavioral signals?
methodology: VN-Index daily data (2015–2024)
MFDFA: 3 features — Delta-alpha, Hurst Exponent (H), Asymmetry (A)
Google Trends: SVI & Search Momentum (Delta-SVI)
Random Forest + XGBoost, SVM, Neural Networks + SHAP explainability
results: Integrated model: F1 = 97.5%, AUC = 0.992
vs. multifractal-only (F1 = 84.7%) or behavioral-only (F1 = 72.3%)
Trading strategy Sharpe ratio: 1.87 vs. 0.94 (buy-and-hold)
Maximum drawdown reduced: 47% to 18%
Proposed 3-tier alert framework for State Securities Commission: Yellow / Orange / Red
